1â# COMP 321: Introduction to Computer Systems
     2â
     3â## Project 6: Web Proxy
     4â
     5â## Overview
     6â
     7âIn this project, you will write a concurrent web proxy that logs requests. A web proxy is a program that acts as a middleman between a web browser and an *end server*. Instead of contacting the end server directly to get a web page, the browser contacts the proxy, which forwards the request on to the end server. When the end server replies to the proxy, the proxy sends the reply on to the browser.
     8â
     9âProxies are used for many purposes. Sometimes proxies are used in firewalls, such that the proxy is the only way for a browser inside the firewall to contact an end server outside. The proxy may perform transformations on web pages, for instance, removing ads from web pages. Proxies are also used as *anonymizers*. By stripping a request of all identifying information, a proxy can make the browser anonymous to the end server. Proxies can even be used to cache web pages, by storing a copy of, say, an image when a request for it is first made, and then serving that image in response to future requests rather than going to the end server.
    10â
    11âIn the first part of the project, you will write a simple sequential proxy that repeatedly waits for a request, forwards the request to the end server, and returns the result back to the browser, keeping a log of such requests in a disk file. This part will help you understand the basics about network programming and the HTTP protocol.
    12â
    13âIn the second part of the project, you will upgrade your proxy so that it uses threads to deal with multiple clients concurrently. This part will give you some experience with concurrency and synchronization, which are crucial computer systems concepts.
    14â
    15â## Part I: Implementing a Sequential Web Proxy
    16â
    17âIn this part, you will implement a sequential logging web proxy. Your proxy must open a socket and listen for a connection request. When your proxy receives a connection request, it must accept the connection, read the HTTP request, and parse the request to determine the name of the end server. Your proxy must then open a connection to that end server, forward the request to it, receive the reply, and forward the reply to the browser.
    18â
    19âYour proxy must be able to support both HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 requests. However, your proxy does not need to support persistent connections.
    20â
    21âYour proxy need only support the HTTP GET method. It is not required to support HEAD, POST, or any other HTTP method, aside from GET.
    22â
    23âSince your proxy is a middleman between client and end server, it will have elements of both. It will act as a server to the web browser, and as a client to the end server. Thus you will get experience with both client and server programming.
    24â
    25â### Logging
    26â
    27âYour proxy must keep track of all completed transactions in a log file named `proxy.log`. Each entry in this log file must be a line of the form:
    28â
    29â```
    30âDate: browserIP URL size
    31â```
    32â
    33âwhere `browserIP` is the IP address of the client, `URL` is the requested URL, `size` is the total size in bytes of the response that was returned by the end server, including the first line, headers, and body. For instance:
    34â
    35â```
    36âThu 16 Apr 2020 20:06:08 CDT: 10.87.76.143 http://www.cs.cmu.edu/~conglonl/ 2584
    37â```
    38â
    39âIn effect, since your proxy does not support persistent connections, `size` is the total number of bytes that your proxy receives from the end server between the opening and closing of the connection.
    40â
    41âOnly requests that are met by a response from an end server must be logged.
    42â
    43âSince opening and closing files are costly operations, your proxy must only open the log file once, during initialization. In other words, your proxy may not open and close the log file each time it writes a log entry.
    44â
    45âWe have provided the function `create_log_entry` in `proxy.c` to create a log entry in the required format. Note that `create_log_entry` does not put a trailing newline on the returned string. Also, note that you are responsible for freeing the memory that stores the returned string.
    46â
    47â### Error Handling
    48â
    49âIn most cases, when your proxy is unable to complete a transaction, it must itself create an HTTP response containing an HTML error page and write this response to the client. This error page must describe why the transaction failed, for example, because the request is malformed or the end server does not exist. The exception to this mandate is when your proxy has already begun forwarding an HTTP response from the end server to the client. Once the end server's response is partially written to the client, your proxy can not send an error page.
    50â
    51âWe have provided the function `client_error` in `proxy.c` to write an HTTP response containing an HTML error page to the client. There are standardized meanings for a small set of the possible `err_num` and `short_msg` parameter values, for example, 400 and "Bad Request" are appropriate when the request is malformed (See RFC 2616 for a complete list). In contrast, there are no standardized values for the `cause` and `long_msg` parameters. Use them as you see fit.
    52â
    53â### Port Numbers
    54â
    55âYour proxy must listen for its connection requests on the port number passed in on the command line:
    56â
    57â```
    58â./proxy 18181
    59â```
    60â
    61âYou may use any port number p, where 18000 <= p <= 18200, and where p is not currently being used by any other services.
    62â
    63â## Part II: Dealing with multiple requests concurrently
    64â
    65âReal proxies do not process requests sequentially. They deal with multiple requests concurrently. Once you have a working sequential logging proxy, you will alter it to handle multiple requests concurrently. Specifically, in this part, you will implement the *prethreading* approach to handling each new connection request (CS:APP 12.5.5).
    66â
    67âTo implement the prethreading approach, you must buffer the new connection requests as they come in. To implement this buffering, you may adapt the `sbuf` that is discussed in the notes and textbook. However, to synchronize access to your buffer, you must use Pthread mutex and condition variables. You may not use semaphores.
    68â
    69âNote that, with prethreading, it is possible for multiple peer threads to write to the log file concurrently. Thus, you will need to ensure that the log file entries are written atomically. Otherwise, the log file might become corrupted.
    70â
    71â## Files
    72â
    73âYour workspace contains:
    74â
    75â- `proxy.c` - Main proxy source file (implement your solution here)
    76â- `Makefile` - Build specification
    77â- `writeup.md` - Skeleton writeup file
    78â- `lib/` - Symlink to csapp library (csapp.h, csapp.c)
    79â
    80âThe provided `proxy.c` file contains:
    81â- `parse_uri` - Helper function to parse URIs
    82â- `create_log_entry` - Helper function to create log entries
    83â- `client_error` - Helper function to write HTTP error responses
    84â
    85â## Notes
    86â
    87â- A good way to get going on your proxy is to start with the basic echo server (CS:APP 11.4.9) and then gradually add functionality that turns the server into a proxy.
    88â
    89â- Initially, you should debug your proxy using telnet as the client (CS:APP 11.5.3).
    90â
    91â- Later, test your proxy with a real browser. Explore the browser settings until you find "proxies", then enter the host and port where you're running yours. You should only set the HTTP proxy, as that is all your code is going to be able to handle.
    92â
    93â- When you test your proxy with a real browser, you should start with very simple web pages. There is a simple, text-only web page at:
    94â  ```
    95â  http://www.cs.cmu.edu/~conglonl
    96â  ```
    97â  Once you get that working, you can try more complicated sites with multiple files and images.
    98â
    99â- You will find it very useful to assign each thread a small unique integer ID and then pass this ID as one of the arguments to the thread routine. If you display this ID in each of your debugging output statements, then you can accurately track the activity of each thread.
   100â
   101â- Be very careful about calling thread-unsafe functions inside a thread.
   102â
   103â- Since the log file is being written to by multiple threads, in order for the output from different threads to not be scrambled, all of the output from a single thread needs to be printed out atomically. Fortunately, the POSIX standard requires that individual stream operations, such as `fprintf` and `fwrite`, be atomic.
   104â
   105â- Be careful about memory and file descriptor leaks. When the processing for an HTTP request fails for any reason, the thread must close all open socket descriptors and free all memory resources.
   106â
   107â- Use the RIO (Robust I/O) package (CS:APP 10.4) for all I/O on sockets. Do not use standard I/O on sockets. You will quickly run into problems if you do. However, standard I/O calls, such as `fopen`, `fprintf`, and `fwrite`, are fine for I/O on the log file.
   108â
   109â- The `Rio_readn`, `Rio_readlineb`, and `Rio_writen` error checking wrappers in `csapp.c` are not appropriate for a proxy because they terminate the process when they encounter an error.
   110â
   111â- The HTTP/1.1 specification does not place an upper limit on the length of a URI. Moreover, in testing your proxy at web sites with rich content, you may encounter a URI that is longer than `csapp.h`'s defined `MAXLINE`. You should explore how `rio_readlineb` behaves when the length of the line being read exceeds the given buffer size.
   112â
   113â- When a br

blocks.text:
Good. So this is the Web Proxy project for COMP 321. The assignment is to:

1. **Part I (Sequential):** Write a basic proxy that:
   - Listens on a port
   - Accepts client connections
   - Reads HTTP requests
   - Parses the URI
   - Connects to the end server
   - Forwards the request
   - Receives the response and forwards it back
   - Logs all transactions to proxy.log

2. **Part II (Concurrent):** Make it handle multiple clients concurrently using:
   - Prethreading approach
   - Thread pool
   - Mutex and condition variables (not semaphores)
   - Thread-safe logging

Key implementation details:
- Parse URIs using `parse_uri` helper
- Create log entries using `create_log_entry` helper
- Write errors using `client_error` helper
- Support HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 but NOT persistent connections
- Strip Connection, Keep-Alive, Proxy-Connection headers
- Add `Connection: close` header
- Handle SIGPIPE
- Use RIO for socket operations
- Keep log file open after initialization
- Handle various error cases

    67âdef find_free_port() -> int:
    68â    """Find an available port for the proxy."""

    76âdef start_proxy(workspace: Path, port: int) -> subprocess.Popen:
    77â    """
    78â    Start the proxy server.
    79â

   104âdef fetch_through_proxy(url: str, proxy_host: str, proxy_port: int, timeout: int = 30) -> tuple:

   106â    Fetch a URL through the proxy.
   107â
   108â    Args:
   109â        url: URL to fetch

   118â        parsed = urlparse(url)
   119â        host = parsed.hostname
   120â        path = parsed.path or "/"
   121â        if parsed.query:
   122â            path += "?" + parsed.query

   125â        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
   126â        sock.settimeout(timeout)
   127â        sock.connect((proxy_host, proxy_port))
   128â

   130â        request = f"GET {url} HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: {host}\r\n\r\n"
   131â        sock.sendall(request.encode())
   132â
   133â        # Receive response
   134â        response = b""
   135â        while True:

   141â            except socket.timeout:

   148â            headers, body = response.split(b"\r\n\r\n", 1)
   149â            return True, body, len(response)
   150â        else:
   151â            return True, response, len(response)
   152â
   153â    except Exception as e:
   154â        return False, str(e).encode(), 0
   155â
   156â
   157âdef _run_proxy_test(workspace: Path, url: str, check_log: bool = True) -> 'TestResult':

   188â
   189â        # Fetch through proxy

   191â
   192â        if not success:
   193â            return make_test_result(False, "fetch succeeded", body.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'), None)
   194â
   195â        # Check log if requested
   196â        if check_log:
   197â            log_path = workspace / "proxy.log"
   198â            time.sleep(0.5)  # Give time for log write
   199â
   200â            if log_path.exists():
   201â                log_content = log_path.read_text()
   202â                if url not in log_content:
   203â                    return make_test_result(
   204â                        False,

   209â            else:

   211â
   212â        # Success
   213â        return make_test_result(
   214â            True,
   215â            f"fetched {len(body)} bytes",
   216â            f"fetched {len(body)} bytes, total {size}",

   220â    except Exception as e:

   222â
   223â    finally:

   232â# ============================================================================
   233â# Basic Functionality Tests
   234â# ============================================================================

   236âdef test_neverssl(workspace):

Actually, wait - looking at the initialization code, I'm calling `Sem_init(&sp->mutex, 0, 1)` where 0 means it's a named semaphore. But that's wrong - I should be using 0 for unnamed semaphore. Let me check the proper usage. In POSIX, sem_init(sem, pshared, value):
- pshared = 0: unnamed semaphore used within the same process
- pshared = 1: shared between processes
